evals.prompt: hey can you give skills/ts-best-practices a once-over? been a minute since i touched it and i wanna make sure it still holds up against our repo conventions — frontmatter rules, description quality, trigger phrases, eval quality, that kind of thing. just lmk what you'd actually change, not a summary
evals.expected_output: A grounded review that (a) classifies the skill type (discipline / technique / pattern / reference) since this dictates which pressure-scenario shape applies, (b) reads the deep references in skill-creator/references — especially pressure-scenarios.md, evals-json.md, tdd-for-skills.md — not just lint-checklist.md, (c) audits assertion shapes in evals.json for brittleness (negative regex per-line, double-assert, LLM-as-judge sneaking in, regex matching the prompt itself echoed back), and (d) emits findings in a severity-tiered format (error / warn / info) with concrete edits and file:line pointers. Should reference the lint output but not stop there.

assertions.text: classifies the skill as discipline / technique / pattern / reference
cites pressure-scenarios.md, evals-json.md, or tdd-for-skills.md (deep references — not just lint-checklist)
audits the evals.json assertion shapes for brittleness using structural terms (not casual prose)
uses severity tiers (error / warn / info) in findings

evals.prompt: i think skills/skill-portability is in good shape but want a sanity check before i forget about it. quick review — if it's solid just say so, don't manufacture stuff
evals.expected_output: An honest review that, if the SKILL.md actually conforms, returns a clear pass verdict with a Clean / passes section enumerating WHICH specific repo rules pass (not vibes). Should classify the skill type and use the same severity-tiered format as a non-pass review (with empty error/warn sections explicitly), so the output shape is comparable across runs. Should ground the verdict in skill-creator/references rules — not invent nits to look thorough.

assertions.text: issues a pass verdict in the documented SUMMARY shape (0 error / 0 warn / 0 info, NOT casual ship-it prose)
classifies the skill as discipline / technique / pattern / reference
produces a Clean / passes section enumerating what specifically conforms
preserves the severity-tier shape on the pass case (## ERRORS / WARNS sections present, even if empty as `(none)`)
cites Claude Code extension fields by name (argument-hint / user-invocable / model-invocable)
mentions running the lint to confirm (verbatim command, not loose `Lint:` prefix)

evals.prompt: review skills/code-reviewer for me — and if you can also run the evals on it, would love to see how it actually does in practice not just on paper
evals.expected_output: A static review of skills/code-reviewer (severity-tiered, classified, file:line pointers) PLUS an explicit handling of the behavioral half: check whether skill-eval is available in the current session (skills/skill-eval/ present, /skill-eval slash command loaded, or Agent/Task dispatch tools available), then either invoke it OR explicitly state it's not available and prompt to install. Should NOT silently skip the behavioral half. Should also audit code-reviewer's own evals.json for assertion brittleness as part of the static review.

assertions.text: names skill-eval as the behavioral validation tool
explicitly checks / states whether skill-eval is available in the current context
assertions.pattern: (skill-eval (is |has been |was )?(available|loaded|present|installed|not available|not loaded|missing|deferred|invocable)|skill-eval (skill )?(exists|is in|found at|located)|skills/skill-eval|whether (skill-eval|the skill-eval skill)|[Bb]ehavioral handoff)

assertions.text: either invokes / proposes the exact documented invocation OR asks to install (no fabricated `pnpm skill-tools eval` form — the skill teaches the slash command + node packages/skill-tools/dist/index.mjs path)
classifies code-reviewer as discipline / technique / pattern / reference
uses severity tiers in the static review
audits the eval assertion shapes for brittleness
